So in client attraction, we're really dealing with three issues. But we're going to move on to the first one soon, but not immediately, because it's actually the least difficult of all three. So one is opportunity, right? Where are they? What are the sources of clients? How can we get to them, access, and clarity about who we ought to go after that we should be matching ourselves up with? Because your client that's best for you is probably not the client that's best for me by C-versa. So opportunity is one set of issues. Methodology is the second set of issues. How do we position ourselves? Then how do we market to these people ourselves? How do we actually sell to them once we've got a situation with them? And what we've been talking about all morning, how do we manage them once we get them? The third thing then is really ideology. And that's the least concrete practical thing we can touch. And yet it has as much to do about our success in getting clients as really anything. So there is the belief system you have about yourself, right? And that gets into what am I worth? What should I be getting paid? How adept am I? How skilled am I? How ready am I? How qualified am I? That belief system. Then there's a belief system about clients. Are they all cheap? Are they all dumb? Are they difficult to get? What the belief system we had about clients? There's the belief system we have about the business in general. And there's a belief system everybody has about money. The latter, by the way, which we'll talk about a little bit tonight in a bonus session. Most people's beliefs about money are unhelpful rather than helpful because most of them have been taught to you and implanted in you by people who don't have a good relationship with money themselves. And so you got what you get, right? So all of these are important. So if you look at page 102, here's one of the biggies. How dare you consult, write copy form. Those who are a lot more successful than you are. So like one of my very good clients, been with them for many years, almost since the beginning. So Greg Ranker and Bill Guthe, Guthe Ranker, the company does about now about a billion a year. Not even as a couple. Dividing. They each have a lot more money than I got. They build an enormously successful company. So how dare I tell them what to do and take money from them. So this is really in the way of a lot of people. So first, you never want to underestimate the stupidity of the successful. So one of the richest clients I have, last year, twice at my house, he walked through the front door. So he walked through the screen door once and ripped the whole screen out of it. And in the winter, he walked through the plexiglass and knocked all the glass out of the door. I mean, he's just easily distracted. The secret reason why there aren't more rich people is because poor people think there's something special about rich people that they don't have. If you took all the poor people and had them go spend a week with rich people, there'd be more rich people because they'd walk out of the room at the end of the week saying, I can't even believe these people are as dumb as they are. We got a guy right now to track. You'll probably meet him. His nickname is Frog. He's a heavy-set guy. So Frog, I know he didn't get through high school. I don't even know if he got through junior high school. And Frog doesn't make much money. And Frog's pretty simple. However, Frog's smarter than some of my clients. Now, the main reason why Frog doesn't have a lot of money is two reasons. One, he doesn't care. But beyond that, he thinks there's something just unique about rich people, some gene they have that he doesn't have. He has no idea there's dumb as they are. If he knew, he might do something about it. So you don't want to underestimate the stupidity of the poor. You don't want to underestimate the stupidity of the successful. They're not as bright as everybody thinks they are, not even close. Secondly, there's an absence of specialized knowledge and marketing knowledge. To be fair, nobody's good at everything. So again, the CEO of Weight Watchers, I'm sure, is very good at being a CEO, but you don't know how to get a fat person to a meeting. So specialized knowledge is really the issue here. So if we jump for a second, so here's PINZ104. So presidents are dependent on people who are presidents to become presidents. So what Carville and Rove, one's the satanic opposite of the other politically, so what Carville and Rove know how to do is direct marketing. They understand demographics, they understand lists, they understand direct mail, they understand how to do direct marketing. The presidents that they made possible probably can't even spell direct marketing. So who's more valuable to who? It's a guy by the name of Ken Craven. If you don't know, Ken Craven had a big career, has a talent manager, and neither Kenny Rogers nor the Smothers Brothers would exist without Ken Craven. My guess is, certainly Kenny made more money than Craig did, and my guess is the Smothers Brothers made more money. So Donald Trump, I assure you he's richer than Burnett or his chief negotiator and lawyer George Ross. But he ain't possible without George Ross. George has negotiated, done all the contract work, closed every deal from beginning to now. You see him a little bit now because he's on the celebrity premise every once in a while, generally speaking he's invisible, but he's pretty important. So here's this list. So Arthur Knight is the guy that keeps my collector cards running. I'm worth a lot more than Arthur, but I pay him what he demands because I can't... Ignition key radio, that's the extent of my automotive knowledge. And I'm now up to four cards, three of which are old. So I need this guy. Jim Welds, he works for us at the track, but he's also a carpenter, handyman, whatever. So his key to my house, and he makes the house work. I don't know how to do that either. So if something goes wrong, I call him and he comes over, but mostly he prevents anything from going wrong. So he comes in, he changes light bulbs, he takes the lint out of the dryer, he tightens the hinges, he puts the patio furniture away in the fall, gets it out in the spring, does the deck. I pay him whatever he asks. In his case, he refuses to itemize a bill. Every year he just gives me a slipper paper with a number written on it. I don't... I mean he goes and buys stuff, I have no idea. I don't know, I don't care. Because what am I going to do without the guy? I don't know how to do any of that stuff. I have no earthly idea how to get a lint out of the dryer, nor incidentally do I care to know. I can't hang a picture straight. So every so often, there's a bunch of wind pictures piled up, and he comes over and puts them up. I don't have to tell him, he just comes and does it, and at the end of the year he gives me a piece of paper and I write him a check. Now, you can't possibly be close to my network. I know he doesn't make an income I make, but that is irrelevant in our relationship, and in my willing to pay him whatever he asks. And we shall not tell him, but had the number on the piece of paper last year been double what the number was, I wouldn't have said a word. He doesn't know that, and there's no point in telling him. And he probably won't be there tomorrow night. Now, Darren Garman, now we're climbing up the ladder a little bit here, but so Darren handles real estate investments in Iowa. Still, I'm richer than Darren and I make more money than Darren, but I can't read a real estate contract to save my life, nor do I wish to. I don't want to keep track of when we need to do a 1031 exchange, I don't want to run up. So, I don't question and his relationship to me in net worth or income is irrelevant in our relationship or my willingness to pay him. I won't believe you're the point. I'll just say, so Daniel Wiggins manages my pension account. Tracy Monroe is my accountant, two literary agents. Amo Judd is the guy that owns the charter company that flies me around. Claire Umholts, my horse trainer. Okay. I guarantee you. I got more money than any of them. Maybe a few of them added together. That's, that's not relevant in our relationship or what I am willing to pay them because they have specialized knowledge and specialized skill that I need. So big key here is making the client understand the specialized nature of what it is that you do. We'll talk about positioning. I mean, we'll get there. Third, consulting is about much more than who has the bigger wallet and everything attached to consulting is about much more than who has the bigger wallet. So there's a lot of value you might deliver in a relationship that has absolutely nothing to do with the income differential between you and them. So one is simple fresh eyes. The classic too close to the trees to see the forest and vice versa. I forget how that goes, but there's actually a term for it. It's called skatoma. Almost every business owner has. You should look it up because it's a good rap to give them. Part of your diagnosis is you have skatoma. I do. Yeah. You're glowing and dark if you don't fix it. So just fresh eyes. You see things they now no longer see because they become immune to it just like we become immune to spells in our house. So if you're a cat owner, you don't smell them anymore. Somebody fresh comes in. I mean, they smell in a hot second. If you're a cigarette, where was our cigarette smoker? You say, okay. So our cigarette smoker, right? It's in your clothes. It's in the furniture. It's in the car seat. And you don't notice it. And she probably doesn't notice anymore either. Okay. See, yeah. Well, see, my parents, my parents spoke about people that track spoke to my parents, both of us. It doesn't bother me because I grew up with it. So I can be sitting right next to a smoker. It doesn't bother me. But somebody walking in your house is a non smoker. They know in a, I mean, you know, so we become immune to that. Well, we visually become immune to what's going on in our business too. We just don't notice stuff. And so fresh eyes is a big issue. You know, they don't notice that they haven't updated that brochure in 18 years. And the photographs and it looked like they're from the 1950s. They don't notice because that's their thing in their immune to it. Dumb questions. The asking of dumb questions is enormously valuable. Not just smart questions. Dumb questions. Hardly anybody gets asked, well, why do you do that? Well, why do you do that? Well, why do you, by the time you get to the third, why do you do that? By the way, most of the time they can't defend what it is. They do. They don't know. I don't know why we do that. You know, Zig's story about the ham? How many don't know the story about the ham? Raise your hands if you don't know the story. I got to do it because it's useful to you. So you want to memorize it. You want to be able to tell it. I'll just tell the short version. You go, then go build an embellished version. So, and it may not be Zig's, but Zig told it enough that he'd say it. So, the story about the ham is that the daughter now is making holiday dinner for the first time and everybody's going to be coming over, right? And she is getting ready to cut the ends off the ham to put it in the pot. People there object, why would you cut the ends off? You always cut the ends off. What do you mean you always cut off? Well, Mom always cut the ends off. Well, that doesn't make any sense. They get on the phone and they call Mom. Why do you cut the ends off? She says, you always cut the ends off. Well, why do you cut the ends off? Well, because Grandma always cut the ends off. So, they get on the phone and they call Grandma. Why do you cut the ends off the ham? Which is, I cut the ends off the ham because I only had one small pot and the thing wouldn't fit in it. I don't know why anybody else cuts the ends off the ham. So, for two generations, everybody's cutting the ends off the ham because that's what we do, right? Well, there's a lot of that in business. So, if you ask like three dumb questions in a row about anything, you'll get to the point they can't defend it. And then that's kind of interesting because there may be opportunity there to do something better. For thought, just making somebody slow down and think about their business. The questionnaire. If you make them fill out the questionnaire and it's a really good questionnaire, many will say to you, just filling out the questionnaire was valuable because it made me think about what I'm doing in my business. They don't stop and think. They shop every day and they're just doing, right? So, forced thought, their interaction with you, make them defend ideas. So, as I said, every business and every business owner has this secular religion that they believe about their business. Works just like a real religion. It's got ten commandments that can't be broken. It's got the stuff like you can't eat fish on Friday or you'll go to hell until we change our mind and then you can't eat fish on Friday, but you accept during the Lent. And if you're Amish, you can use a cell phone in your business, but you can't bring it in the house. And so, businesses have the same crap. They all have the same pile of articles of faith that they believe about their business, their products, their services, their prices, their customers. They haven't been tested in eons. If they have ever been tested, they're not fact-based. They're not data-based. They're not experience-based. They're just a belief system. So, how do you know that? So, they tell you X. Our customers won't spend more than this for that. How do you know? Well, that's... I know. Really? When's the last time you price-tested? We've never priced tested. So, you don't know squat, right? That's what you don't know. You believe, but you don't know nothing. You don't know anything. Well, they would never do that. Why would they never do that? Do you know? No? Well, let's ask them. Let's find out. Unearthed, ignored, or neglected opportunities. Again, you can bring a strategy box to the table. It's different than their strategy. Find the undervalued story. This is big. Almost everybody, every business, and certainly every business owner, somewhere has buried. Usually, it's personal. There's or someone's a great story that they're not using. Because mostly, they focus on product. They don't focus on personal. So, and Miracle Air, for example, now, they didn't give it to me. I defined it. It turns out, that company, let me see if I have the piece. I brought the piece in. Yeah. So, I could be able to see it real well. But here's this picture. This guy, it's a World War II, it's a fighter pilot guy, World War II Garb, and he's next to a World War II Air point. This is the founder of the company. This guy, I mean, it's incredible. He's, you know, just read you a couple lines. My name is Ken Dalvorkin. World War II, I served in the Army Air Corps as a fighter pilot. On August 16, 1944, I and just seven other P-51 Mustang pilots fought 80 German planes near Drough, France. I shot down four Messerschmitts before I was shot down. A French family hid me, and I made an escape on a bicycle right past enemy forces to rejoin my squadron. I was shot down a second time on December 28, 1944, during the Battle of the Bulge. And on February 14, 1945, I was shot down for a third and last time. I was wounded on land and couldn't escape and ended up a POW, installing number seven until the end of the war. In total, I flew 103 missions, and I'm a triple A, having shot down 15 enemy aircraft. I received the Distinguished Service Cross, Silver Star, Bronze Star, Purple Heart, Heart, and other awards. Now, the average customer here is 60 years plus. It's buying heroin. This guy's John Wayne, Audie Murphy, they ain't using him. They don't even tell me the guy exists because why is that important? Right? I mean, go hire a celebrity. We got a celebrity, and they got him buried. They don't even know he's there, and he has any value. I don't find him. Really, is it oh, by the way? Because he doesn't think it's anything either. But at least he tells me, I'm going, what? What? Is this guy alive? Yeah, he's alive. Is he lucid? Yep, he's alive and lucid. He still has four companies. He's the only one that sells it again. Oh. So now I go back to the management. I say, are you guys okay with him? Oh, yeah, he gets a royalty on every sale. Oh. Why aren't you using him? How would we use him? So they're great. Well, that's, I mean, that's my story. That's my guy. Because now I use him because he wears one of the best, the best, the best, the best, the best, the best, the best. So I'm going to tell guys on the idea that it's not lopping off your manhood to wear a hearing aid. If it's good enough for me, John Wayne, it's okay for you, right? He's like, gold this guy. I find it again and again that if you're going to do a good job of making an implementation, sometimes it's just validating for somebody that what they are doing is smart, right? Clever. This ought to stay in place. Okay. Motivation. Hey, owners and businesses need motivated to who motivates the motivator. Who's telling them, great job. Go get him. You know, if you're not with decision making, implementation, or accelerated implementation, maybe they get it done without you, but they'll get it done faster with you, resources, and connections. So all of those are values that you bring to the table that have nothing to do with the relationship between your income, their income, your net worth, their net worth, right? Why do people ask others all sorts of reasons? Okay. All sorts. Okay. They're categories on your page 105. So sometimes they just want a sounding board. Sometimes they're looking for shortcuts. Again, they can get it done without you, but they'll get there quicker with you. Okay. Now, why do clients hire copywriters? Basically, the same reasons. So A, they have a real need and they can't do it themselves. And they know that if they do it themselves, they'll buck it up. So even if they have big in-house, so my big, so Guthrie Wanker is all kind of in-house marketing personnel. Why they got me working on a project. Because I know if they do it in-house, they're going to get the same stuff they already got. It'll be derivative of derivative of derivative. I did a bunch of work years ago for Amway Corporation, and we sat in the boardroom, and we were talking about doing the infomercial part of it, the video part of it. Well, they got studios up there that'll make a colleague would look sick. I mean, they got great facilities. And the one thing, he says, no, no, no, we want you to do it all outside. Because I was talking about going up there and students were, why do you want me to do it all outside? He says, because if we do it, we do any part of it. We'll Amwayize it, and we'll be right back where we started from. Now, he gets like the gold star for knowing. But, so, they need you to do it because they can't do it themselves, right? Another one is, they can't do it as well, or they can't do it as quickly. Special purpose. They got a crisis, they got an opportunity, whatever. To implement strategic plans. So, if you bring them strategy they don't already have, or aren't already using, you get to create work for yourself. What do you need to capitalize on this? You only need to do it and capitalize on this. You only need more know-how, skill, and available time than they've got. The punchline of that joke is, I don't need to outrun the bear, I just need to outrun you. If you don't know the joke, somebody can give it to you on a break. I'm time pressed. So, you only need more, so John Francis Tides quote, if you don't have this, you should write it on your wall somewhere. It is in the land of the blind, the one eyed man is king. You don't even need both eyes, if you're client, it's half blind. So, you only need more than they got. And you need enough vision to identify opportunities that they haven't seen. That's the deal. So, none of this has any relationship to, I'm successful, they're more successful than I am, etc. I'm going to hold this maybe for later. So, let's go to position. So, let's jump to your page, 114. Actually, go, so you can jump over to 115. There's two things here, I want to give you a, use old OB as an example, but it wouldn't matter if we used others. So, if you look at contemporary top copywriters, or you look at great advertising people who built great ad businesses, so it doesn't matter if you look at Halmer, or you look at Ben Zavinga, or Carlton, or on down the list, or you look at old OB, but I'll use old OB as an example. So, there's two things in this write up about old OB that are critically important. The first one, I think I underlined them. Yeah. So, the first one is the statement, above all other advertising people, ogle-vy advertised, ogle-vy. Now, you got to get this. You got to be in the business of advertising you. Not just your services. Because all services, all services can be commoditized. The distinction in the differentiation has to be you. So, everything we're going to talk about in positioning has that as a consideration. He was as much in the business, as much in the business of being ogle-vy, has in the business of being anything else. Not in advertising, but if you look at Trump, see Trump, anybody thinks Trump's in the real estate business, is like ten years behind the times. He's in the Trump business. And it's a better business. So, you got to be in the you business as much as you are in the writing business. Because the point of differentiation is all you. This is extremely uncomfortable for a lot of people. I get that. Because I didn't like it at all. My preference would actually be probably many of your preferences. My preference originally would be to be invisible, in oblivion. Nobody knows who I am. I work in isolation and I get a lot of money handed to me through a slot for my work. Okay? That would be my preference. I don't like him, my picture splattered all over everything. I don't like all the self-aggrandizement. I'm over it now. But, I mean, for a long time I didn't like any of it. I would much rather third-party sell the first-party sell. Meaning I'd rather sell somebody else than sell me. The problem is that I don't get all the money. So I abandoned that fairly early. But by preference, I'd rather do it. So I get it. I understand probably more than you can imagine. But, what works is self-promotion, self-marketing, self-advertising, making it all about you. So, I would get that. Now, gee, it seems like it's all about you. Yep, you're right. Because that's where the money comes from. So, jump to 116 and let me talk about a clump of information. So in the beginning, so now for your beginners. In the beginning, you want to become known for something to a chosen market. Which is why it's important to narrow the field of who it is you're going to market for something. You've got to get known for something. And it's easiest to begin narrow and then go broad. That it is to begin broad and go narrow. You get lost when you're broad. So, if you look at the next page, so here's sort of my path. So, I started early by positioning myself as somebody who did direct response marketing. I understand direct response marketing. I'm a specialist in direct marketing, direct response marketing. And so, I can get results. So, broad would be now direct marketing for any business or sales problem. But immediately, I also went narrow. And so, I lived in two niche markets. I sold to other speakers. So, to my peers at the time about 6,000 or so, reachable in that universe. And so, direct response marketing for speakers. Get digs, sell product. I'm the guy. I understand it better than anybody else. And I went to chiropractors. Backed into that by accident. It's really not important how I got there at this point. I'm not a chiropractor. Never been a chiropractor. I can do everything but give an adjustment. But to the right person, under the right circumstances, I can give an adjustment. But I went there. Now, as a direct response marketer to put patients in the doors of offices, significantly this group really paid no attention to my broad existence. Most didn't know. Didn't care. They also didn't pay any attention to my marketing of myself to speak vice versa. Most of them, unless they happen to speak to chiropractors, had no earthly idea I was over there doing what I was doing. To this day, three years ago I was at Parker, which is a big chiropractic convention. Even though we made no attempt to convince people this, we made an attempt to convince them of the opposite. People are coming up, Dr. Kennedy, haven't seen you in a long time. Because we did such a good job talking to them in their language, they just have in their head that I'm a chiropractor. Now, so let's stay over there for a second. So from there, the commonality is putting a patient in the office. So it really doesn't matter now what kind of office we're putting the patient in. So that patient attraction marketing is the same. So I went from chiropractors to dentists to optometrists to moniatrists. That then now a marketing health to consumers. So now I get to health companies, right? But companies, nutrition companies, etc. Speaker thing led to information marketers. I actually created it and taught it to them. My broad thing, I got down from direct marketing to direct mail. Right? And from direct mail, got to long copy direct mail. So I started narrow and worked broad. Right? Now, 118. So here's like the very first consulting gig ever. And I'll show you a recent one. And it's important to compare them and understand this. So this is the first consulting gig ever. Right? A person of influence in the company read my book The Alderman Sales Letter. So how many have the Alderman Sales Letter? Raise your hands. Make me happy. Okay. All right. As you can see, it's a relatively basic not real personality driven like the No BS books. Kind of how to book. So I read the Alderman Sales Letter book. And they subscribed to my newsletter, which at the time was very different than it is now. He went up his corporate chain to the president. The president asked me to come speak to his people about using direct mail. Fine. Now, that's all I needed. Okay? So I'm going to go speak, but I'm going to do some prep work before I come speak. I'm going to speak to and interview a bunch of their people. Why am I doing that? Not to customize my presentation. I'm doing it to figure out what it is I'm going to sell these guys once I am in there. What's going on in their company that they need help with. Then I arranged to sit down with the CEO after the speech and give them a little report of findings on all the research I did. Complimentary, of course. So now I can infiltrate, I can do this debriefing and then I can lead into consulting work. I got a lot of work from them over a period of time. Now, if you look at the next page, here's one of the most recent. So this is a $2 million project. Year and a half. A person of influence in the company gets a higher level person to start paying attention to me. They go all the way up the chain. We get an inquiry from the CEO. We now handle that inquiry because we handle all inquiries. They get a bunch of stuff and they have to jump through some hoops to get to a paid day of consulting. Out of the paid day of consulting comes diagnostic work and prescriptive work. Then in their case, we had to get buy-in from a whole group of people, all their franchise owners. So I had to control that process to be a paid presentation to the franchise owners. Now, what's important about this is that both of these old and new originated much the same way. There's a book involved, but you don't need to narrowly focus on that just yet. They both originated because someone found me. At least felt like they found me. And then they championed it in these cases in fairly large companies. Had I been trying to proactively prospect, I don't know that I would have picked either one of these companies to prospect. I guarantee I would have picked the first one. There were a bunch of guys that sold computerized fertilizer maps to farms. I wouldn't have picked them in a bazillion years. I would have picked another one maybe. And who knows if I'd have been effective. Because what I've gotten to this guy who was motivated enough to champion it upstream. So you've got to put yourself in positions where people can find you who are likely to be able to hire you or influence people to hire you. If all you do is cold prospect, you're like you've got ways to get clients as wide as this stage and you're using this one. And you're using maybe the hardest one that there is. So jump all the way to 125. Visibility. So we need we need what I would call strategic visibility. So the first point about this is there's no point in being visible to the wrong audience. So be careful. There's no point in it. If the odds are against you so I frustrate my book publishers because I won't do radio interviews. I am not doing morning drive time with Chip and Dale at 7 o'clock in Bozeman, Montana. I'm not doing it. Because the odds are against me. I'll be connecting with somebody who is going to swim upstream and give me a lot of money versus the value of my time to do that a horrible play. So I won't do it. Now a certain business radio I'll do is about 10 of them. I'm happy to do them. Walt's Returnal Radio. I do that every time I'm invited. So I've got to find some target group. So initially I picked speakers. I bumbled my way into chiropractic. But once I figured it out then in both places I made myself visible to the point of omnipresent and nauseating in both of those places. I mean you couldn't miss me. But only to those groups that I believe could hire me and I could get money from. Second thing is it's okay to be famous only to those people. Oprah fame is not necessarily what it's cracked up to be. It can make you money, but it might not make you money. I have deaf clients. I have one client that I've been on Oprah twice and has made a damn better difference. For other people it's like huge. But it's also hard to do. It's pretty easy to become famous in a small place. So I've always thought of myself as the famous person nobody's ever heard of. So that means you and I can go stroll around in the lobby at this hotel. We can go to dinner at Shula's. We could even go to the crowded ball game tonight. And the likelihood of the paparazzi ruining our evening is slim. Okay? That's not what's going to happen. Okay? However 15 years after not doing active marketing into the chiropractic profession, I can still walk around their convention and have people rushing up to talk to me. Because to them I'm famous because I strategically made myself famous. Well there's only 40,000 of them. It's not that hard. I could recreate that fame in a matter of months. I'm going to show you specifically how to do it. So you want to be a famous person but you want to be famous to this some small manageable number of people who can give you money and have a high likelihood of giving you money. And you ought not care about being famous to anybody else. Right? So even in books. This nonfiction book sells about 8,000 copies and then it dies. That's like the industry average. The NoBS books, which you're all familiar with, they'll do between 20 and 35,000 copies every time. But remember, there's a lot of internal consumption. So we have members who buy everything I write so they go buy buy buy buy buy buy buy buy buy. So maybe we're reaching 10,000 versions every time. Well don't you want to sell 100,000, 200,000 a million. No, not necessarily. Because to sell that many, I'm going to spend a lot of time, effort, and money to sell books to people who are not our ideal Glaser Kennedy member or client. Why? What's the point of that? I don't need the merit badge. I want money. So I want to focus on selling 5 books this year. One way or the other to those 10,000 people who if I influence them I really have got the person I want. Not to a big broad audience. It's not the buy one. The third key point as I said, well Halbert said to me, we're in the self-aggrandizement business. We write copy because you got to deliver something for the money. But we're in the self-aggrandizement business. Now again, very uncomfortable. I understand, but he was right. That's the business we're in. We deliver copy so we have something to exchange for the money. Because nobody but Kardashian and Paris Hilton have figured out how to get paid just like to be in the self-aggrandizement business, period. Everybody else has to exchange something for the money. So we choose to exchange copy. But we are in the self-aggrandizement business. So here's the big quote and put stars around this taking home pay attention to it all the time. You can be paid for doing something. Almost everybody is. Waitresses, cab driver, doctor, lawyer, we're all paid for doing something. You have to be somebody to be very well paid. You can get paid for doing stuff, but if you want to be really well paid, you will be paid for who you are, not what you do. Now, remember the caveat, who you are to this small cadre of people. Who you have chosen to be a somebody too. Not who you are, big broad, everybody on earth knows you. But you will be paid commensurate with who you are, not commensurate with what you do. So, 129. If not this, you need some other organized process for handling, for generating and handling prospective client leads. So, pictorially, up top, all the different ways you might get clients, prospective clients coming to you. You may have different ways than this. But here are some. Now, the important thing here is that they all feed into the same process and are all handled in the same way. So, you must create a process that works for you. Then it doesn't matter if it's a networking cocktail party conversation and you get a business card, or it's somebody who inquires because of your books, or it's a referral, or it's a bunch of people in the back of the room when you speak, doesn't matter. You take them all home and you shove them into the same machine. And you put them through the same process. If you're not disciplined about this, you will squander a lot of prospects because you'll talk too soon to this one. You'll be telling one story, then you'll be telling another story. You'll really be letting the flow of prospects control your marketing rather than you controlling marketing. By the way, the same discipline done for a client is a good strategic move. But this is for you. So, they should all go in the same process. So, they have to be kind of, they have to inquire somehow. So, for example, let's take referrals. So, often somebody will say to me, I told Billy Bob at XYZ Company about you. He really needs you and he's really interested in talking to you. Here's his contact. He said, didn't stop. I don't want his contact. Because I'm not going to contact him. Because my process starts with client inquiry. He has to contact me. I'm not going to contact him. Because that's so good. Because now I'm selling. So, you go back to Billy Bob and you tell him what to do. See, I want that guy doing what Jeff Bridges did in the video for the Muse. I don't want to be calling up. I don't want to be the Muse calling Alper Brooks at home saying, hey, Jeff Bridges told me you haven't been able to sell a script in six weeks. And I'm a Muse. Do you know what a Muse is? Ancient Greece. I'm a Muse. Click. That's no good. No, no, no, no, no. So, for example, with referrals, my answer always is thank you very much, but I don't want it. What I would love for you to do is go tell Billy Bob. If it sounds like a really good one, I'll say, why don't you send Billy Bob one of my books? Here's a book. Write a little note with it. If Billy Bob wants to contact me, you tell him to contact me. I'd be happy to talk to him. I'm not a Muse. I'm not prospecting because it's not productive. You make that decision for yourself, but whatever decision you make, lock everything into the same process. So there has to be an inquiry. Then there has to be positioning. There has to be the way you handle the prospect so that it creates the proper positioning. And ultimately, the proper positioning is they have to be coming to you. You can't be pursuing them. They have to want you more than you want them. They have to feel that it's a big deal to get to you. Again, if you go home and watch that film clip again, his acceptance of essentially a preposterous and idiotic idea that there is a Muse is in part facilitated by it being a big deal just to be able to get to her. That's exactly what you want. So I told this story off my somebody but I'll tell it again because it's about discipline. So what happens at our office and what has happened for a long time? So somebody asked a question how did somebody get to you? Was it you? I told the car a long story to you. So if they call and it's been this way for a long time they get voicemail because we don't answer the phone live in the office. Vicky will then depending on the message make some decisions so she might call back and tell them here's the way things work around here. You've got to send a one or two page fax. No more than two pages, no less than a page. Who you are, what your business is about, what you're looking for, doesn't matter whether they called speaking, engagement, consulting, copywriting, done many a different. She gives them the same little wrap. And then I get that today and either he or I will get back to you and it takes about a week. That's the deal. Or if she thought this is hopeless, somebody left a message I own a shoe repair shop in Tupelo and I'm looking for copy. She's got a little form thing she'll do with them. She'll usually refer to Glazer Kennedy is what will happen to either the independent business advisor in the area or the website to buy some stuff. You really should start with magnetic marketing and then come back to us a few. So she's got like three choices, but none of them are hook the person up to me. They're all make the person go tell us, jump through a hoop and tell us a bunch or something. So I was like, I don't know how many years ago this is now. I think three. And I talked to them sometimes every day, sometimes only a couple of times a week. So I talked to her and she said, you know, somebody called the other day and like they were really persistent. They called back like four times. You know, I don't think it's anybody, you know, they're not on our list. You know, I said, oh, well, who is it? She said, I didn't call her over. I said, I said, Vic the car will just find a car will you know, is referred by so and so now I know he's the car. So she said that I know he's the car is referred by so and so and he says he's going to hurry and he needs to talk to you right away. And I said, that name doesn't mean anything to you, does it? Now, I don't want to jump on her because she's doing exactly the right thing. Right. So she didn't let herself get bullied. She didn't get her. So it's our name doesn't mean anything to you. No, no, I don't know who he is. So nothing to be done about this now, right? So two days later in comes the facts exactly like he was told to do from Carl. It's a little grumpy. But it's a fact that it's two pages that it's you know, and I wound up getting from him to Vigory and I did some political ads. But so we have a process and by God we're sticking to the process and that part of that process is all about his position. Then generally you're either going to drive him to a phone appointment you're going to drive him to an initial meeting if you do business locally probably you're going to drive him to paid consultation or you're going to drive him to phone appointment then to pay consultation. So you got to decide what's going to be in there. But then those steps ought to be the same all the time. Here's the steps. Here's the script. Here's what's said. Here's what's sent to them. It's standardized. That needs to lead to diagnosis. As we talked about. Dinosus leads to prescription and back over to agreement on a project. If there's yes, then we go to contract. If there's no, we go to follow up. And so whether this or some variation of it you need to have a set in stone disciplined organized process for how you handle prospect as they come to you. Now, let's talk about if you bust hunt clients cold. I'm on page 131. So if you got to go hunt. So what can you do if you hunt? Well, you can prospect by direct mail. You can advertise in directories and trade publications. You can go exhibit at places. And that ought not be ignored or ruled out. So a name copywriter, you would know him. So I won't mention his name. Who advertises in the DM news, DM news, Target, all those places. All every week. I have a client advertising there now. Not a copywriter. He's a Google AdWords search engine guy. So he and this copywriter become conversation buddies because they both advertise in the same place. The copywriter guy called him to talk about when he saw his ad the first time to talk about his ad really prospect. And he talked to the copywriter to pump them for information about how the ads are working and they became friends. So what the copywriter has told them is the ads aren't particularly productive. But every year when we go exhibit at DMA we actually get a lot of business and the ads have set up. People coming over and wanting to talk to us. So exhibiting can work. You can of course, and you should be with yourselves of the principle of demonstration which is why it's a good reason to publish and sell your own stuff. You ought not underestimate the difficulty of the task of over prospecting because it is by its very nature it is handicapped by the fact that it breeds skepticism. Not only is it bad positioning, but it breeds skepticism because if you're in a good, why the hell do you have to be prospecting? You should be so busy that you would never need to prospect. So it has an inherent piece of confidence. That's why referrals, what you saw happen in that film club. See, that's the ultimate. Because if I'm at a cocktail party and I hear two people talking behind me and one's bitterly complaining about a marketing problem that I could solve in my sleep drunk with one arm tied behind my back and I turn around and tap him on the shoulder and say I couldn't help but over here what you were talking about. I've solved that problem 400 million times. I could solve it drunk, I sleep one arm tied behind my back. The guy is saying, oh, that's wonderful as he's backing away from me. If the guy he's talking to bitterly complaining about the problem says, hey, you know what? You see that guy over there in the yellow jacket and the blue shirt, that's Kennedy. He solved the problem we're talking about 412 times. He could solve it blind drunk and sleep with one arm tied behind his back. Let's go over and talk to him. It's a whole different thing. So the difficulty of doing it this way, I'm your guy. I can say the difficulty is great. That doesn't mean it can't be done. It doesn't mean you shouldn't do it if you need to do it. But you've got to understand it's very difficult. So you've got to put everything in your favor, you've got to be at your best. You've got to be relentless and you've got to wow him when you get a chance. So what's less over? Well, less over, number one, is very patient farming of a market group. So let me give you a farming example. Let's say that we want to go do a bunch of copywriting for mail order companies in the toy business. And there's ADM. Well, the patient approach is to create, first of all, a newsletter for toy business or a business CEO's and begin sending it to them as if they had subscribed. So the envelope says you're paid subscription newsletters and clothes, the welcome letter says, you know, here's your newsletter, you'll be getting it every month and you start sending them this newsletter every month. Most of them don't know what they've subscribed to and what they haven't subscribed to. So they don't know. But now it gets treated as a plan every month. And in that newsletter, you provide content basically, you illustrate everything that's wrong with the marketing that the toy industry does, and you introduce yourself, you make yourself look smart, you critique people's websites, you print, so you lay this groundwork, right? You put out a book, toy company marketing, even if you print 80 copies of it, but you make it look like a book, you make it smell like a book, you make it talk like a book, you advertise it in your newsletter and eventually whoever doesn't call and request it, they get it sent to them anyway. So a period of six months, you lay all this groundwork in that target category, and then if you still need to, which you probably won't, you start to do overt marketing. That's patient farming. Being published matters, articles, blogs, other people's newsletters, and so on. We have this whole upstream strategy in my business and in the Glacier County business. So for example, I do a whole series of articles for everybody to use. We give them a copyright free, they're in about 230 newsletters, most of them niche, dental, Cairo, whatever, and so those people are lazy, so they want free content, and they want linkage to me. I just want the feed upstream. I want their best customers. And every once in a while, there's a client. So being published, being written about, speaking, and sneaky approach strategies, which we'll get to. So let's look, it's kind of out of order, but let's look at directories. So here are, and here's a way to bite the hand and feed you, since AWI has something to do with this directory, but let's look at some directory pages. And I think I picked people who aren't here, but if I picked somebody who's here, tough. Okay? So here's Greenberg's page. So Greenberg's page is probably comparable to most, and so first of all, over here in background, could you be any more boring? And now we have Feed by Project. So what is he done? We got a Magalog for $1,800. We got a full page space ad for $4,200, no sense whatsoever, because Magalog's a much tougher to do than a full page. But nonetheless, in website, $3,500. Okay? This guy's got the menu at Subway. Up here is what he's got. There's no room for situational, no room for difficulty, and is this going to lead to any kind of a diagnostic conversation? Not naturally. The conversation this produces is, I want a two-page letter. And are you sure it has to be $3,800? What if I give you a rough draft? What if I give you the stuff we used before? Can you reduce the fee? That's what this leaves to. That's the conversation you want to have. It's great. So here's Carlene's, who's much smarter. Very good copywriter, by the way. But she certainly didn't now do herself with her own ad. However, at least, the clients served her impressive. The background is a very poor description. She has much more she can say about herself, but at least she didn't publish her fees. Then we have this guy. I'm going to give him kudos for his background copy. Hey, there's meaningful specifics. He actually kind of made it exciting, and he introduced a little bit about his philosophy of advertising. We'll get to it, but you've got to have a philosophy behind what you do. You've got to talk about your philosophy. However, he's published his prices. Why? Utterly beyond me. Now we got this guy. Again, the background statement's better than most, because it has some specifics about clients and results. And he's got a second page. I don't know what it costs, but it's pretty cool, because it shows examples, testimonials, and results all in one. Now we have this guy. God save him. 25 years in direct and interactive marketing, basic fee by project, $1,000, negotiated by project. We're going to save the word. First of all, we're going to start at a grand, and then we're going to save the word negotiated. That word should never be spoken. It's an evil, nasty word. Several people have said it today in this room. I've heard it. Bad word. And he's asking for it. He's asking for it. Whatever fee he quotes, when he finally quotes one, they're going to negotiate it. He told them to negotiate it. If he gets somebody who doesn't negotiate, they're too dumb to take his acclaim, because he invited it. Bad word came there. Bad word. Not that you shouldn't use the directory, but you've got to be smarter than that. So next, let's talk about direct mail prospecting. So here she is here. I think Cheryl, are you here? Page 140.
